segments.s455-g57: به فرمان او is an adverbial prepositional phrase meaning 'at/by his command' (به used in the sense 'by/at'). رفت is the past form of 'go' used here in a modal construction; in this poetic order the verb رفت precedes the modal باید, so رفت باید together mean 'must go'. همه is the subject 'everyone'. The overall order is topical adverbial (به فرمان او) + verb + modal + subject, a poetic inversion of ordinary Persian.
segments.s455-g58: که introduces a subordinate (causal/explanatory) clause 'for/because'. او is the personal pronoun 'he'. چون is the comparative/simile 'like/as'. شبانست is a contracted poetic form of شبان است = 'is a shepherd' (شبان + the copula -st). و simply links the second nominal phrase. گردان رمه is a nominal phrase meaning 'one who turns/drives/tends the flock' — گردان here functions as a verbal/agentive notion attached to رمه ('flock'), yielding 'the flock-driver/keeper' (poetic usage).
segments.s455-g59: This is a nominal list (بزرگی و شاهی و لشکر) followed by وراست, where وراست functions as a predicate meaning 'is right/appropriate' or 'befitting'. In other words, 'greatness, kingship, and an army are fitting (for him)'. The nouns are coordinated, and وراست is an older/poetic copular/adjectival word.
segments.s455-g60: بدو = 'to him' (a shortened dative), کرد is the past of 'to do/make' and here 'کرد باید' forms a causative/obligative idea 'must be done/must be given'. همی is an adverb meaning 'always/continually' and پشت راست is an idiomatic noun phrase 'straight/firm back' meaning 'firm support' or 'backing'. The literal order 'بدو کرد باید همی پشت راست' = 'to him must be made (i.e. given) always a firm backing.'
segments.s455-g61: به شادی here is an adverbial phrase 'with joy' or 'in rejoicing' (به used adverbially). خروشی is 'a cry/clamour' and برآمد is the past 'rose/issued forth'. ز کاخ is the poetically contracted prepositional phrase 'from the palace' (ز = from). Word order places the adverbial and the noun before the verb, then the source phrase after the verb, a common poetic arrangement.
segments.s455-g62: که introduces the explanatory/temporal clause ('for/when'). نورسته is an adjectival past participle 'newly sprung/newly grown' modifying شاخ. دیدند is the past plural 'they saw'. فرخنده is an adjective meaning 'auspicious/fortunate' and شاخ is 'branch' (here a bough/branch as an omen). The adjectives and noun are arranged in close sequence (adjective + noun) in poetic style.
segments.s455-g63: ببردند is the past plural 'they carried/they bore away'. چندان qualifies the extent 'so much/so many' (i.e. to such an extent). ز هر سو is a circumstantial phrase 'from every side', with ز = 'from' and سو = 'side/direction'. نثار is the direct object 'offerings/gifts'. The clause describes the immense quantity taken from all directions.
segments.s455-g64: که here introduces a result clause 'so that'. شد is 'became' (past of شدن). ناپدید is the predicate adjective 'vanished/invisible'. اندران is a locative/relational particle 'in/among them/therein' and شهریار is the subject 'the king'. Poetic word order places the predicate before the locative and the subject, yielding 'so that the king there became unseen.'
segments.s455-g9: نشسته is a past-participial/predicate meaning 'seated' and precedes the noun as is common in Persian poetry; ستاره‌شمر is a compound noun 'star-counter/astrologer' serving as subject; پیش here is the preposition/adverb 'in the presence of, before'; شاه is the definite patient/role 'the king'. Word order is poetic but corresponds to 'The astrologer, seated, before the king.'
segments.s455-g10: ز اختر is a prepositional phrase 'from the stars' giving the object of the gaze; همی کرد is the classical poetic progressive/past habitual construction (همی + کرد) meaning 'kept doing' or 'was continually doing'; روزی here is lexical in the line but the provided sense entries don't include the usual 'one day'/'a while' sense, so it is best treated as part of the temporal/nuancing element attached to the verbal phrase; نگاه is the noun 'gaze/look' which is the thing done. The whole clause reads 'and from the stars he kept looking' (i.e., he kept his gaze fixed on the stars).
segments.s455-g11: به introduces a dative/benefactive direction, here marking recipients or addressees; شهریور و بهمن appear as a coordinated pair of names (the second following without an explicit conjunction, a common poetic compression); از بامداد is an adverbial prepositional phrase 'from dawn' that frames the time of the action. Word order places the temporal phrase after the recipient phrase in typical epic diction.
segments.s455-g12: جهاندار is the agent noun 'sovereign' (subject); داراب را uses the object marker را to mark Darab as the definite direct object/recipient; بار here is a concrete 'load/burden' or figurative 'charge' given to someone; داد is the past tense 'gave' (subject + object + verb order: agent–object–verb, typical in Persian). The phrase means 'the sovereign gave Darab a burden/charge.'
segments.s455-g13: یکی is an indefinite numeral 'one' meaning 'one (of them)'; جام is the object 'cup'; پر + سرخ یاقوت is a compound describing the cup as 'full (پر) of red (سرخ) rubies (یاقوت)' — in classical Persian adjectives and modifiers often follow the head noun; کرد is the past tense 'made' or 'filled' and completes the clause: 'One filled a cup full of red rubies.'
segments.s455-g14: Another parallel clause begins with یکی دیگری 'one (person), the other' where the verb is omitted (ellipsis) because it repeats کرد from the previous clause — a common poetic device. پر ز یاقوت زرد literally 'full from/of yellow rubies' uses ز as the partitive/source marker 'of/from' and zرد 'yellow' modifies 'yaghoot' after it; the omitted verb is understood: 'one (made) the other full of yellow rubies.'
segments.s455-g15: چو آمد is a subordinating temporal clause 'when he came' (چو = 'when'); به نزدیک is a locative prepositional phrase 'to/near'; ایوان فراز places the location, with فراز ('on high' / 'elevated') modifying ایوان ('portico') after the noun as is common in Persian poetic syntax. Together: 'When he came near the lofty portico...'
segments.s455-g16: همای آمد is the main clause 'the Homa (mythical bird) came'; از دور is an adverbial 'from afar'; و connects the next action; بردش is the past of بردن 'to take' with the enclitic object pronoun -ش meaning 'him/it' (so 'took him/it'); نماز (literally 'prayer') is the noun object associated with بردن here. The literal reading is 'the Homa came from afar and took his prayer,' with بردش composed of برد + pronominal suffix.
segments.s455-g89: کنون is an adverb meaning “now/presently.” اختر گازر is a nominal phrase: اختر “star” followed by گازر, a participial/attributive form from گذر/گذشتن meaning “passing” (literally “the passing star”); in epic poetry such compounds are compact. اندرگذشت is the 3rd-person past of اندرگذشتن (archaic/poetic) meaning “passed/went by” (the prefix اندر- is an older prepositional/vernacular element), so the clause says that now the passing star has gone by.
segments.s455-g90: به is the preposition “to/towards/into” introducing a direction; دکان here means “shop/booth/occupational place.” شد is the past 3rd-person singular of شدن; in older Persian narrative it can mark arrival or turning to a place (roughly “went/arrived/turned to”). و is the coordinating conjunction “and.” برد in this clause functions as the past of بردن “to take/bring” (so it governs the direct object اشنان), اشنان means “those people/those ones,” and به دشت is the directional phrase “to the plain.” Together the second half says he went to the shop and took those people out to the plain.
segments.s455-g49: بفرمود is an honorific/polite third‑person past verb meaning 'he said/commanded' (literally 'he deigned to say'). تا here is the subordinating conjunction 'so that/that' introducing the reported clause. خواندند is past plural ('they read/chan­t/uttered') and takes آفرین (an interjection) as its direct object — together: 'they cried "afarin".'
segments.s455-g50: به introduces the beneficiary or target of the acclamation ('for/to'). شاهی is used as 'kingship' or 'the kingly one' (here 'the king'). بران is a demonstrative/adverbial 'that/thence' linking back to the person just mentioned. نامدار زمین is a two‑word epithet: نامدار 'renowned' modifying زمین 'the land/country'.
segments.s455-g51: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when/as'. The prepositional phrase بر تاج شاه ('on/upon the king's crown') precedes the verb in typical poetic order; خواندند is past plural 'they cried/sang' with آفرین as the utterance.
segments.s455-g52: بران here functions adverbially ('thereupon'/'thereon'), linking the action to the previous clause. تخت 'throne' is the place word, followed by the preposition بر 'on/upon' and گوهر 'gems/jewels' as the thing scattered; افشاندند is past plural 'they scattered/strewn'. The subject is the verb's -ند ending (they) and is omitted explicitly.
segments.s455-g53: بگفت is past 'he said/told'. آنک is a relative pronoun 'that/what/which' introducing the object clause; اندر نهان کرده بود is a perfect past construction: نهان 'hidden' with کرده بود (compound past) yields 'had hidden (away) inside'. Word order places the relative + locative phrase before the verb, as is common in classical Persian.
segments.s455-g54: ازان 'from that/from it' introduces the reason or source. کرده here functions as a nounish 'deed/act' (the act just mentioned). بسیار 'very/much' modifies غم 'sorrow' and خورده بود is a compound past idiom ('had eaten/been afflicted by') meaning 'had suffered' or 'had much sorrow' — بود is the auxiliary past.
segments.s455-g55: بدانید is an imperative/presentive 'know' or 'be aware' addressed to the audience ('know that...'). کز is the fused relative/conjunction 'that/from which' introducing the following noun phrase. بهمن is a proper name here (Bahman), and شهریار is a title 'king' attached to it.
segments.s455-g56: جزین is a compound 'except this/other than this' (جز +‌ این). نیست is the negative copula 'is not/does not exist'. اندر جهان 'in the world' is the locative phrase, and یادگار 'memorial/memento' is the predicate noun — together: 'there is no memorial in the world except this.'
segments.s455-g1: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' marking the place/source; درگاه پرده is a noun phrase 'the curtain of the portal/threshold' (درگاه = portal/threshold, پرده = curtain). فروهشت is a past tense verb 'let down / spread out' (placed before the subject as in classical Persian), and شاه is the subject 'the king' placed after the verb.
segments.s455-g2: به یک هفته is a temporal phrase 'for one week' (به + time expression). کس را is the definite/indefinite object 'anyone / a person' with the object marker را; ندادند is the negative past plural 'they did not give' and راه (lit. 'way') here means 'admission/entrance' — together ندادند راه = 'they gave no admission / admitted no one.'
segments.s455-g3: جهاندار زرین is a noun with postpositive adjective: 'the sovereign (جهاندار) golden (زرین)'. یکی تخت places the numeral 'one' before the noun 'throne' and تخت is the object; کرد is the past tense verb 'made' agreeing with the subject جهاندار.
segments.s455-g4: دو کرسی is simply 'two seats/thrones' (numeral + noun). ز (poetic از) marks material or composition 'of/from', so ز پیروزه و لاژورد means the seats were of/covered with turquoise (پیروزه) and lapis-lazuli (لاژورد); و is the conjunction 'and'.
segments.s455-g5: یکی is the numeral/indefinite 'one/a certain'; تاج پرگوهر is a compound noun phrase 'a crown full of gems' (پرگوهر = 'full of jewels / jeweled'); شاهوار is an adjective 'kingly / regal' following the noun, giving a nominal descriptive phrase (no verb expressed).
segments.s455-g6: دو یاره enumerates 'two yāreh' (here best read as 'two attendants/companions' or two items associated with the king). The clause continues with یکی طوق گوهرنگار where یکی = 'one (of them)' and طوق گوهرنگار = 'a necklace/collar (طوق) adorned/engraved with gems' (گوهرنگار functioning as a postpositive adjective). The copula is omitted, typical in listing.
segments.s455-g7: همه جامه خسروانی is a noun phrase 'all the royal garments' (همه = all; جامه = garments; خسروانی = royal). به زر is a prepositional phrase 'with gold' indicating the material or ornamentation used.
segments.s455-g8: درو is a contracted classical sequence (در او = 'in it / into them') referring back to the garments; بافته is the past participle 'woven' (resultative/passive sense: 'were woven in them'). چند گونه modifies گهر: 'چند گونه گهر' = 'several / various kinds of gems.'
segments.s455-g73: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. داراب is the proper name (subject). از تخت کی is a genitive phrase 'from/at the throne of Kay' (تخت … کی = 'Kay's throne'). گشت شاد is a compound verb phrase: گشت (became) + شاد (happy), i.e. 'became happy' — an ordinary past/resultative use of گشت meaning 'became'.
segments.s455-g74: به آرام here functions adverbially ('peacefully' / 'in calm') with به forming an adverbial phrase + آرام. دیهیم is an adjective/noun 'of the villages / the rural people' (the populace of the countryside). بر سر نهاد uses the past stem نهاد (from نهادن 'to place') with بر سر 'upon the head / over' — idiomatically 'set/established (over)' so the whole phrase means 'he set (peace) over the rural folk.'
segments.s455-g75: This is a simple coordination: زن (woman/women) و گازر (and passersby) — the poet repeats گازر for emphasis — آمد (came) is past tense and دوان is an adverb/participle 'rushing/running' modifying آمد; word order is subject(s) + verb + manner.
segments.s455-g76: بگفتند is the past plural 'they said/cried out'. کای is the vocative particle 'O' (address marker); شهریار جوان is the noun phrase being addressed: 'young king' (شهریار 'king' + جوان 'young').
segments.s455-g77: نشست کیی is a nominal phrase 'the Kayanian seat/session' (کیی = Kayanian); بر تو is the dative 'upon you/for you'. فرخنده باد uses فرخنده 'auspicious' + باد, the optative particle 'may (it be)', so the clause is a blessing: 'May the Kayanian seat be auspicious for you.'
segments.s455-g78: This is a parallel optative clause. سر 'head' is the direct object; بدسگالان 'the ill‑omened / ill‑fated ones' functions as a genitive/epithet (i.e. the bad people/enemies). تو here emphasizes that this benefit is for you. کنده باد uses the participle/ adjective کنده ('uprooted / torn off') with باد as optative 'may ... be torn off', so: 'May the heads of your ill‑omened foes be torn off.'
segments.s455-g79: بفرمود is an honorific past 'he commanded/said'. داراب is the subject (the commander). ده بدره زر is a numeral + poetic modifier + noun: ده 'ten', بدره 'moonlike / full‑moon (i.e. bright, poetic epithet)', زر 'gold' — together 'ten bright (moon‑like) pieces of gold' (a poetic description of gold pieces).
segments.s455-g80: This clause gives what was to be brought. بیارند here is the verb meaning 'they should bring / bring (them)' (the expected sense is 'bring'), but that specific sense is not listed in the provided options for this item. پرمایه is an adjective 'rich, substantial'; جامی is 'a cup/goblet' and گهر is 'a gem/jewel', together meaning 'a richly gemmed cup' or 'a cup full of jewels.'
segments.s455-g17: برافشاند is a past (3rd‑person) verb meaning “scattered/strewn” with the verb placed before its object in this poetic order. آن is the demonstrative/definite article “that/the,” and گوهر شاهوار is a noun phrase “regal/lofty jewel/pearl” (گوهر = jewel, شاهوار = regal/lofty) serving as the direct object of برافشاند.
segments.s455-g18: فرو ریخت is a compound verbal phrase: فرو (adverb/prefix “downward”) + ریخت (past of ریختن “to pour/fall”), so together “fell/poured down.” از دیده خون is a prepositional phrase “blood from the eye” (از = from, دیده = eye, خون = blood), and برکنار functions adverbially “to one side/aside.” The whole segment describes blood pouring down from the eye to the side.
segments.s455-g19: پسر را marks the definite object with را (“the son” as direct object). گرفت is the past (3rd‑person) of گرفتن “to take/seize.” اندر آغوش تنگ is a locative/prepositional phrase “into a close/tight embrace” (اندر = in/into, آغوش = embrace, تنگ = close/tight); adjective follows the noun in this poetic ordering.
segments.s455-g20: ببوسید is a past (3rd‑person) verb “he kissed.” و simply coordinates the next verb. ببسود here is an archaic/uncertain form not well matched by the provided sense list (contextually it functions as a second past verb meaning roughly “he pressed/embraced”), and رویش is the possessed noun “his face” (روی + ـش = his face). به چنگ is an adverbial phrase “to/toward the breast/hold” (چنگ literally “claw/hand” but used metaphorically as the place of pressing).
segments.s455-g21: بیاورد is a past (3rd‑person) verb “he brought.” و links the clauses. بر تخت زرین is a prepositional phrase “upon the golden throne” (بر = on/upon, تخت = throne, زرین = golden). نشاند is an archaic past transitive meaning “he seated/placed (him)” — the object (the son) is understood from the previous clause.
segments.s455-g22: دو چشمش is “his two eyes” (دو = two, چشم + ـش = his eye/eyes) and ز here is the causal/ablative poetic preposition “from/because of.” دیدار او is “the sight/meeting of him” (دیدار = seeing/meeting, او = him). خیره ماند is a two‑word predicate: خیره (staring/fixed) + ماند (past/remained) = “remained fixed/staring.”
segments.s455-g23: چو here means “when” (a temporal conjunction). داراب is the proper name (Darab). بر تخت شاهی is a locative phrase “on the kingly throne” (بر = on, تخت = throne, شاهی = of kingship/kingly), and نشست is the past (3rd‑person) verb “sat/took his seat.” The phrase describes the moment Darab sat on the throne.
segments.s455-g24: همای آمد is “the Humay (mythical bird) came” (همای = Humay, آمد = came). و coordinates the following action. تاج شاهی به دست is “the crown of kingship into/into his hand” (تاج = crown, شاهی = kingship/royal, به = to/in, دست = hand); the clause means the Humay presented/placed the royal crown into his hand.
segments.s455-g33: اگر introduces a conditional “if.” بد کند is the verb phrase “does evil / behaves badly” (بد + کند, present/neutral third-person). ز + و (classical poetic spelling zū = ز او) means “from him / toward him” (poetic), and مگیر is a negative imperative “do not take / do not seize.” آن به دست is a prepositional phrase “that into (your) hand” where به + دست = “into the hand / into one’s power.”
segments.s455-g34: که here introduces the explanatory clause “that / for that reason.” جز تخت = “except the throne.” هرگز مبادت is an optative/negative jussive construction (مبادَ + ت) meaning “may it not be / let it never happen,” and نشست is the verb “to sit”; together the phrase means “may it never happen that he sits (anywhere) except on the throne.”
segments.s455-g35: چنین is an adverb “thus/so.” داد is the past verb “gave” (he gave). پاسخ is the direct object “answer,” and به مادر جوان is the indirect object “to the mother of the young man” (مادر + جوان; here جوان functions as the noun “young man,” i.e. the son).
segments.s455-g36: که introduces reported speech: “that …” تو هستی is “you are” (تو + هستی, second-person present). از گوهر is a genitive/partitive phrase “of the essence/of the very substance,” and پهلوان is the noun “champion/hero”; together: “that you are by essence a hero.”
segments.s455-g37: نباشد here is the negative of باشد used impersonally in an optative/conditional sense ('it would not be'); شگفت is a noun meaning 'a wonder' and follows the negated copula in the idiom نباشد شگفت = 'it is not surprising'. ار is an archaic short form of اگر ('if'). دل آید به جوش is a normal subject–verb–prepositional phrase: دل (subject) + آید (3sg present/subjunctive 'comes' / 'should come') + به جوش ('into boiling' / 'into a surge/passion'), giving the conditional clause 'if the heart comes into a boil'.
segments.s455-g38: به یک بد تو is a prepositional phrase meaning 'for/at one bad [deed] of yours', with تو marking the possessor/topic. چندین is an adverb/adjectival quantifier 'many/several' placed before the interrogative چه داری 'what do you have' (داری = 2sg present 'you have'). خروش is the direct object 'uproar/cry'. The whole line asks rhetorically why you make so much noise over a single bad act.
segments.s455-g39: جهان‌آفرین ('world‑creator') is the subject or vocative; از تو is a prepositional phrase here meaning 'with/regarding you' (classical Persian uses از for the English 'with' in such predicates). خشنود is the predicate adjective 'pleased/delighted'. باد at the end is the classical optative particle 'may' that gives the whole clause the meaning 'May the Creator of the world be pleased with you.'
segments.s455-g40: دل بدسگالانت is a genitive construction 'the hearts of your bad‑fated/ill‑omened ones' (بدسگالانت carries the 2nd‑person possessive suffix -انت). پر دود is a predicate phrase 'full of smoke' (پر = 'full (of)' + دود = 'smoke'). باد again is the optative 'may', so the clause reads 'May the hearts of your ill‑fated ones be full of smoke.'
segments.s455-g81: ز = 'from' (poetic preposition). هر جامه‌ای = 'each garment' (هر + indefinite ـای). تخته is the direct object 'boards/plaques' placed before the verb because of poetic inversion. فرمود is 3rd-person past used as 'he ordered/commanded'. پنج is the numeral modifying تخته; the object and its numeral are split across the verb by poetic word order, so the whole means 'he ordered five boards from each garment.'
segments.s455-g82: بدادند is 3rd-person plural past 'they gave' (verb carries the subject). آنرا is the definite direct object marker + pronoun 'that/it'. که here introduces a causal clause 'because'. او دید رنج literally 'he saw suffering' = 'he had suffered' and explains the reason they gave it. The subordinate clause explains motive: 'they gave it because he suffered.'
segments.s455-g83: بدو is an indirect-object phrase 'to him' (not the verb 'run'). گفت here functions as past 'said' (3rd-person). کای is an archaic vocative 'O', and گازر پیشه‌دار form an address 'passer-by, tradesman' (two nouns in apposition). Together: 'He said to him, “O passer-by, tradesman...”'.
segments.s455-g84: همیشه = 'always'. روان را: روان 'spirit/soul' as a definite object, marked by را. به اندیشه دار is a compact verbal phrase: به اندیشه 'in/to thought' + دار (here an archaic/poetic imperative/verb meaning 'keep, have' = keep in mind). So the clause commands: 'always keep your soul/mind in thought / be ever mindful.'
segments.s455-g85: مگر begins a conditional/exclusion 'unless'. زاب صندوق = 'a chest at/from the Zab' (place-name + noun). یابی is a 2nd-person verb form 'you find' (here subjunctive/optative possibility after مگر: 'unless you can find'). یکی = 'one/a single' modifying صندوق. Word order is compact and poetic; literal: 'Unless at Zab you find one chest...'
segments.s455-g86: چو = 'like/as' marking a comparison. دارا here is the name/epithet (classical context: Darā) or 'wealthy/possessor' used as the point of comparison — in this line it functions as a proper-name-like reference. بدو = 'to him' (dative). اندرون = 'inside/within'. کودکی = 'a child' (object). Together the phrase describes a chest 'like Dara's, with a child inside to give to him.'
segments.s455-g87: برفتند is 3rd-person plural past 'they departed/left'. یک لب literally 'one lip' (here idiomatically 'a mouth'), پر از آفرین 'full of praise' (پر = 'full (of)', followed by از + noun). Poetic word order places the descriptive phrase after the verb: 'they departed, a mouth full of praise.'
segments.s455-g88: ز = 'from/of'. دادار = 'the Creator' (a divine epithet). بر شهریار زمین = 'toward/to the king of the land' or 'for the king of the earth' (بر here = 'to/toward', شهریار = 'king', زمین = 'land/realm'). Together: the praise ran 'from the Creator to the king of the land' — i.e. their acclamation ranged from God up to the sovereign.
segments.s455-g41: Literal order is somewhat inverted for poetic effect: ز من (from me) + یادگاری بود (was a keepsake) + این سخن (this saying) — i.e. “This saying was a memento from me.” بود is the past copula (‘was’). ز is the poetic form of از (‘from’), and این سخن is postposed for emphasis.
segments.s455-g42: که introduces a subordinate clause qualifying the foregoing ‘saying.’ هرگز + نگردد is a negative present/subjunctive/optative of گردیدن (here ‘become / be turned / be entered’), giving a sense of ‘shall never be / will never come to be.’ به دفتر کهن means ‘into/on the old book/register,’ with به expressing direction/target.
segments.s455-g43: آفرین کرد (lit. ‘made/uttered praise’) is a simple past clause: ‘(he) uttered “bravo.”’ فرخ همای is the agent phrase ('the fortunate Homa' — epithet + name). برو at the start is an archaic/exclamatory particle here introducing the action (a poetic/colloquial flourish rather than the literal imperative ‘go’).
segments.s455-g44: که introduces reported speech/explanation: تا جای باشد = ‘so long as a place remains’ (تا … باشد = temporal/purpose clause). تو بادی به جای is an address to ‘you’ (تو) with بادی functioning as an optative/subjunctive-type form meaning ‘you shall be’ or ‘may you be’ in that place; به جای (‘in that place’) is the locative. Overall: ‘that, as long as there is room, you shall be in place.’
segments.s455-g45: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past verb meaning ‘he commanded/ordered.’ تا introduces a purpose/result clause (’so that / that’). موبد موبدان is a title-like/construct phrase: ‘the mobed of mobeds’ = ‘the chief priest’ (reduplication to show rank).
segments.s455-g46: بخواند is the verb in the dependent clause governed by تا after a command; here it means ‘to summon / call’ (not merely ‘to read’). ز هر کشوری = ‘from every country/land,’ where ز is the poetic form of از. بخردان is the plural agent noun ‘the wise ones / wise men’ (object of the summons).
segments.s455-g47: هم از لشکر introduces an additional group (‘also/from the army/host’). آنکس که is a relative phrase ‘those who / the ones who,’ and بد نامدار is a compound/epithet meaning ‘ill‑famed’ or ‘badly renowned’ (بد here negates or gives a pejorative sense to نامدار).
segments.s455-g48: This is an epithet phrase describing those people: سرافراز (‘proud,/triumphant’) modifies شیران (literally ‘lions,’ often used metaphorically for brave men). خنجرگزار is a compound agent noun ‘dagger‑bearer’ (one who carries/wields a dagger), giving them a fierce/armed character.
segments.s455-g25: بیاورد (present/past-poetic form here = 'brought') is the verbal action; the coordinating و links the two clauses. The prepositional phrase بر تارک او ('on his brow') precedes the verb نهاد (past of نهادن, 'placed'), so the clause literally orders as 'brought and on his brow placed.' نهاد is the finite past verb completing the clause.
segments.s455-g26: جهان را carries the object marker را (definite object) so 'the world' is framed as the (definite) direct object; به دیهیم او is a prepositional phrase indicating recipient/audience ('to his villagers/people'). The verb phrase مژده داد ('gave/announced news') comes at the end — literal order: 'gave news to his people to the world' — i.e. 'announced the good news to the world and to his people.'
segments.s455-g27: چو ... گرفت is a temporal clause introduced by چو = 'when.' از تاج دارا is a genitive/prepositional phrase 'from Darā's crown' (از = 'from' + تاج دارا = 'Darā's crown'); فروزش گرفت is the verb phrase ('took/was imbued with radiance') with گرفت as the past tense verb placed at the end.
segments.s455-g28: هما is the subject (the mythical Huma bird). اندران کار is a locative/relational expression ('in/among that matter' — اندران = 'in that/therein', کار = 'matter/affair'). پوزش گرفت is a compound verbal idea: پوزش ('pardon/excuse') + گرفت ('took/obtained'), i.e. 'obtained (or sought) pardon' — verb again at the end.
segments.s455-g29: به داراب is an indirect-object prepositional phrase ('to Darā'). گفت is the past-tense reporting verb 'said/told' (here used as a verb of speaking). آنچ اندر گذشت is a noun clause functioning as the direct object: آنچ = 'that which', اندر = 'in/within', گذشت = 'passed/transpired' — together 'what transpired.'
segments.s455-g30: چنان دان is an instruction/imperative-like clause: چنان = 'so/thus', دان here means 'consider/hold' (take it to be so). که introduces the subordinate result/explanatory clause. بر ما همه places the outcome 'upon us all' (بر = 'on/upon', ما = 'us', همه = 'all'), and بادگشت is a nominal abstract meaning 'reversal/turning (of fortune)' that serves as the content of that clause.
segments.s455-g31: This clause lists subjects before a single past verb: جوانی و گنج آمد = 'youth and treasure came/arrived' (آمد is the past-tense verb placed after the coordinated nouns). After that, و رای زن appends another nominal phrase 'and the woman's counsel/opinion' — رای (opinion/counsel) followed by زن (woman) is a genitive-like construction 'the woman's counsel.' The verb آمد is understood to cover the latter phrase as well.
segments.s455-g32: This is two short nominal predicates joined by و: پدر مرده = 'the father (is) dead' where مرده follows پدر as a predicative adjective/participial adjective, and شاه بی‌رای‌زن = 'the king (is) without the woman's counsel' — بی- attached as a prefix yields 'without', and رای‌زن is a compound 'the woman's counsel' (here used adjectivally with بی-). No separate finite verb is needed in poetry; the predicates are understood.
segments.s455-g65: جهان (subject) + پر شد (predicate): پر here is an adjective 'full (of)' and شد is the simple past of شدن 'to become', so the clause reads literally 'the world became full (of)'. از + noun marks the material or cause: از شادمانی و داد = 'of joy and justice'. The coordinated nouns شادمانی و داد are the contents filling the world.
segments.s455-g66: کی را is a topicalized/object-marked interrogative ('who' + را marking the person concerned). نیامد is the past negative of آمدن 'to come' used idiomatically with یاد to mean 'did not come to mind / did not remember'. ازان رنج یاد = 'the memory of that suffering'. Together it functions as a rhetorical question: 'who did not remember that suffering?'
segments.s455-g67: همای آن زمان puts a proper name with a temporal modifier ('the Homa at that time'); گفت is the past-tense verb 'said/spoke'. با موبدان is a prepositional phrase 'with the mōbeds/priests' indicating interlocutors. Word order is subject/adverbial — verb — complement (typical in classical Persian narrative).
segments.s455-g68: که‌ای is a poetic/vocative formula (که + ای) used to address someone: 'O (you who are) ...'. نامور and باگهر are epithets 'renowned' and 'jeweled'; بخردان is formed with ب‑ + خردان (the wise) giving a dative/associative sense 'to/among the wise'. The whole phrase is a vocative line addressing a celebrated, gem‑adorned person among the wise.
segments.s455-g69: به سی و دو سال expresses duration 'for thirty‑two years' (به + number + سال). آنک کردَم uses آنک ('that which') + کردم (past 'I did') to refer back to what was done, and به رنج is an adverbial phrase 'with hardship'. Together the clause says 'I spent/acted thus for thirty‑two years with hardship.'
segments.s455-g70: سپردم is the past transitive 'I entrusted/handed over' and is followed by بدو 'to him' (dative). پادشاهی و گنج are the direct objects 'kingdom and treasure'. The verb precedes the indirect object (typical in Persian narratives) and the list of objects is joined by و.
segments.s455-g71: شما شاد باشید uses باشید (present subjunctive/optative) as a polite benediction: 'may you be happy'. و links to فرمان برید: فرمان 'the command' + برید, here an archaic/poetic verb form meaning 'heed/obey' or 'execute'. The clause is an injunction combining a wish and a command.
segments.s455-g72: ابی رای is a compound epithet modifying رای ('counsel/opinion'); in classical usage ابی‑ often has a pejorative or descriptive nuance (here 'bad/false' or 'worthless' counsel). او is the possessive 'his', یک‌نفس is an adverbial phrase 'for a single breath / for even a moment', and مشمرید is an archaic/obsolete imperative form meaning 'do not heed / do not follow' (negative imperative sense). The whole clause means 'do not, even for a moment, follow his counsel.'

sentences.s455-s29: Everyone must go at his command, for he is like a shepherd and the one who tends the flock.
sentences.s455-s30: Greatness, kingship, and an army are fitting for him; one must likewise give him constant, firm backing.
sentences.s455-s31: A shout of joy rose from the palace, for they saw the newly sprouted, auspicious branch.
sentences.s455-s32: They carried off so many offerings from every side that the king there became invisible (was lost to sight).
sentences.s455-s5: The astrologer sat before the king and kept his gaze upon the stars.
sentences.s455-s6: From dawn the sovereign gave Darab his burden for Shahriyar and Bahman.
sentences.s455-s7: One made a cup full of red rubies; another (made) the other full of yellow rubies.
sentences.s455-s8: When he came near the high portico, the Homa came from afar and took his prayer.
sentences.s455-s45: Now the passing star went by; he went to the shop and took those people out to the plain.
sentences.s455-s25: He commanded that they cry 'Bravo!' for that famed king of the land.
sentences.s455-s26: When they cried 'Bravo!' over the king's crown, they thereupon cast jewels upon the throne.
sentences.s455-s27: He told what he had hidden away inside; from that deed he had suffered much sorrow.
sentences.s455-s28: Know that, of Bahman the king, there is no memorial in the world except this.
sentences.s455-s1: The king drew the curtain at the palace gate, and for a week no one was admitted.
sentences.s455-s2: The golden sovereign made a single throne and two seats of turquoise and lapis-lazuli.
sentences.s455-s3: One jeweled crown, regal in appearance; two attendants/companions — one (being) a jewel-studded necklace.
sentences.s455-s4: All the royal robes were woven with gold and in them were woven many kinds of jewels.
sentences.s455-s37: When Darab joyfully came to the Kay's throne, he peacefully established calm among the villagers.
sentences.s455-s38: Women and passersby came running and cried, 'O youthful king!'
sentences.s455-s39: May the Kayanian seat be auspicious upon you; may the heads of your ill‑omened foes be cut off.
sentences.s455-s40: Darab ordered that ten shining pieces of gold be brought and a richly gem‑set cup.
sentences.s455-s9: He scattered that regal jewel; blood poured down from the eye to one side.
sentences.s455-s10: He took the son into a close embrace; he kissed him and pressed his face to his breast.
sentences.s455-s11: He brought him and set him on the golden throne; his two eyes remained fixed at the sight of him.
sentences.s455-s12: When Darab sat upon the kingly throne, the Humay came and placed the crown of kingship in his hand.
sentences.s455-s17: If he does evil, do not take that into your hand; may it never be that he sits on anything except the throne.
sentences.s455-s18: Thus he answered the mother of the youth: “You are of the very stuff of a hero.”
sentences.s455-s19: It would not be surprising if the heart were to boil; for one bad thing of yours, why have you so many cries?
sentences.s455-s20: May the Creator of the world be pleased with you; may the hearts of your ill‑fated ones be full of smoke.
sentences.s455-s41: From each garment he ordered five boards; they gave it to him because he had suffered.
sentences.s455-s42: He said to him, 'O passer-by, tradesman, always keep your spirit in thought.'
sentences.s455-s43: Unless you find at the Zab a single chest—one like (that of) Dara—containing a child for him.
sentences.s455-s44: They departed with mouths full of praise—from the Creator to the king of the land.
sentences.s455-s21: This saying was a keepsake from me — that it should never be recorded in the ancient book.
sentences.s455-s22: Then the fortunate Homa cried “Bravo,” saying that so long as there is a place, you shall occupy it.
sentences.s455-s23: He commanded that the chief priest summon the wise men from every country.
sentences.s455-s24: Also from the host, those men who were ill‑famed — the proud, dagger‑bearing lions.
sentences.s455-s13: It (the bird) brought it and placed it on his brow, and announced the glad tidings to the world and to his people.
sentences.s455-s14: When (she) took the radiance from Darā's crown, the Huma, in that affair, obtained/asked for pardon.
sentences.s455-s15: He told Darā what had happened; consider it so — that a reversal (change of fortune) may come upon all of us.
sentences.s455-s16: Youth and treasure arrived, and the woman's counsel; the father was dead, and the king was without counsel from the woman.
sentences.s455-s33: The world was filled with joy and justice, and who did not remember that suffering?
sentences.s455-s34: At that time the Homa spoke with the mōbeds, saying, 'O famed one, jewelled among the wise,'
sentences.s455-s35: For thirty-two years I endured that with hardship; I entrusted to him the kingship and the treasure.
sentences.s455-s36: May you be joyful and heed the command; do not, for even a single breath, follow his counsel.
